result:
There is a peculiar kind of silence that exists at the bottom of the ocean, a silence not of emptiness, but of immense, crushing pressure and ancient patience. Down in the hadal zones, where sunlight surrenders miles above, the water itself becomes a heavy blanket, pressing in on all sides. Down there, the clock of the world ticks differently. Eons pass like lazy afternoons, unhurried and unbothered. 

I find it profoundly comforting that there are places on this planet entirely indifferent to human endeavor. While we scramble across the surface, building our monuments to permanence, arguing over borders and beliefs, the deep ocean continues its slow, rhythmic breathing. Hydrothermal vents belch sulfurous smoke into the freezing dark, sustaining ecosystems that defy our understanding of life. Ghostly fish with bioluminescent lanterns drift past mountains of obsidian, entirely unaware of the chaotic world above. 

It is a reminder that we are not the center of the narrative. We are brief sparks, flashing momentarily against a backdrop of deep time. When I feel overwhelmed by the noise of the present—the endless scroll, the urgent notifications, the creeping anxieties of modern life—I try to imagine that deep-sea silence. I picture the abyssal plains, vast and undisturbed, holding their quiet vigil. It doesn’t make my problems disappear, but it shrinks them to their proper proportion. In the grand, dark, beautiful ocean of time, we are just ripples on the surface, soon to be smoothed away, leaving no trace, into the endless, peaceful blue.
